sentences.text: OK.
F-E--
S-A-R-A.
My name is Sarah Fershko.
S-A-R-A, Sara.
F-E-R-S-H-K-O, Fershko.
My given name was Spitzman.
S-P-I-T-Z-M-A-N.
It makes me 78 years old.
In Lutsk.
L-U-T-Z-K.
It's in Ukraine now.
When I was born, it was Poland.
And I was raised in Poland.
About 40,000, maybe more.
Oh, there were-- I can't give you a number.
But it was a Jewish-- a big Jewish population in Lutsk.
There were professionals, a lot of professionals, a lot of tradesmen.
And it was also a commercial city.
It was the capital of Volyn.
So it was an industrial city.
And the people made a living.
And as at one time, politically-- and the Jewish use, as Jews everywhere like to study, however couldn't study himself or belong to an organization where they studied.
The Yiddish language was a-- a live one.
And Yiddish literature was alive.
And we spoke Yiddish.
Even if I-- I went to a Polish school.
In the house, I spoke Yiddish.
I learned myself to read and write in Yiddish only taking from my father of the ABC, I mean, the alphabet.
And it's very easy for me.
It came easy to me to learn.
So I have read a lot.
And the [?
Jews ?] was very well-read and politically very, very ripe in the-- in the young age.
I was musically inclined.
So I didn't have any special drive for politics.
So I was-- as a young child I started-- when I was 6, 7, I started to play the violin.
And my parents did the utmost to teach me, because I was talented.
When I was 14 years old, I still went to school, to public school.
I played in the symphonic orchestra in Lutsk.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and then I perpetuated my musical studies.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I grew older, I learned even a trade.
My-- one brother of mine was a photographer.
And he had a studio.
So I learned a trade in order to go to Warsaw and to perpetuate my studies and to be able to work, to support myself.
And I did, but I went too late.
It was in-- in 19-- I went in the end of 1937.
So I was there 1938.
And it was already before the war.
And-- and it was very unpleasant for Jews.
Yeah.
Yeah.
My parents' names were, my father was Mosze Spitzman.
And my mother was Rysia Tokman, maiden name.
My father was a designer of ladies shoes.
When I grew up, he was-- he had only a store and sold shoes.
And he had one worker who was sitting and making shoes for ladies.
It was a specialty that my father designed.
It was like custom, before they started to bring in from factories, shoes.
So he was a specialist.
But he was a very social person.
He belonged to the shul.
He was on the board of the shul.
He was president of [INAUDIBLE].
This was a special organization to help poor people medically.
There was a doctor and a nurse.
And-- and the [? youths ?] then, who belonged to [INAUDIBLE], played theater.
The money that they made went for the-- for the organization.
We had groups that-- in Europe, it was very popular to go into the street and to-- and to sell, like a flower or a pin.
You know, like, from paper flowers, made the pin.
And with-- with [NON-ENGLISH], with-- with the-- the-- and the money went to it.
We made annual balls.
And the money went for the organization.
And it was very popular.
It wasn't a political, but it was a social [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] place where-- where people could come, whoever could pay $0.50.
Then it was 50 [INAUDIBLE].
If he couldn't pay, he was helped anyway.
No, my mother was a homemaker.
And she had six children to support.
So she was a wonderful-- a wonderful hostess.
And I-- I can't remember our home without people.
We have-- always we had people in the house.
And there were meetings.
And there were a lot of plays.
And all the money that was made went to-- to-- to some cause.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's how I was raised, with a real Jewish education.
We were-- we were not Orthodox.
But it was a traditional home with Shabbas was Shabbas, and yontif was yontif.
And that what I-- I am raised with.
And I live with it until today.
Yeah.
We were six children.
Four--
Four-- we were four sisters and two brothers.
My sisters' names was [INAUDIBLE], Riwka, Rachel, Leya, like the [INAUDIBLE].
And-- and the brothers was Isik and Zeylik.
Very, [? barely. ?] I remember when I was three that my mother's father lived with us.
I even don't remember the name.
And she told me that I was [? fitting ?] him with-- with tea or something.
Very, very vague.
Oh, it was very traditional.
And the-- the-- when-- when mother lighted the candles, we were all around.
And then when father went to shul, when he came back, and the table was set for Shabbas, and-- and he made kiddush, it-- it lives in us.
We-- we can't get rid of it.
It's lives in us.
And-- and I am not talking only about the food, but the atmosphere at the table, the singing.
My mother sang beautifully.
My father sang beautifully, and my brother.
We-- we were a musical family.
And my father was very able.
He-- he could make rhymes from a word.
You said a word, and he made it in a rhyme, a [? furls, ?] a whirls or I don't know.
It came in.
It's like I was growing up.
I said this is the way it should be.
And the same thing with yontif.
Every Passover, every Rosh Hashanah, we were at the table.
The-- the-- the holiday was explained to the children.
And-- and we soaked it in.
And that's why we became Jews.
And in any-- in any situation that we-- that we have been, we found ourselves later, we remain Jews.
In Poland, it wasn't a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'll tell you only one thing.
When I went to school, I went to a public school.
I was sitting on-- on the same bench [BACKGROUND NOISE] with a-- with a Gentile girl.
And we became friends.
Like-- like a schoolgirl, she used to come to us.
And she was eating with us.
And I helped her to make the-- the lesson.
She was a little bit not too bright in some subjects.
I helped her.
We made together.
When the war broke out, she turned from me.
She didn't want to look me into the eye.
And she went away.
Isn't it a-- and other, in Poland, it was different.
In Poland when at Christmas, the Jews were in the house.
They were afraid to go out to-- it's not here.
This is a commercial Christmas.
But in Poland?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
You-- you come now to Poland, there are no Jews.
But they will invent the Jews to be anti-Semitic.
And that was going on.
And we were used to it.
And we-- we studied.
And you know, not me only will tell you that they were, in the universities, left benches, or left sides, where Jews only could stand.
And then they-- they didn't let them in all together.
But the Jews can't be idle.
They worked on themselves.
And they-- they created a beautiful Jewish literature.
And we had many, many newspapers and journals and weekly journals in Jewish with beautiful, beautiful literary work.
Can you imagine another people who took a language like zargon, Yiddish.
It was German and-- and English, all the-- all the languages.
And they transform it into a literary, beautiful speaking language until today.
The whole world literature was translated into Yiddish.
And many, many writers, Yiddish, were translated in many other languages.
We have an example, like Bashevis Singer.
He became-- he got a Nobel Prize for Yiddish.
He didn't write in-- in another language-- in Yiddish.
It was translated into English.
But he'll wrote in Yiddish.
He even spoke in Yiddish when he-- when he got his Nobel Prize.
I knew him very well.
He used to come to every concert of ours, being here or New York.
I'll tell you we lived as we could.
And we went on with our daily work, with studies, with practicing.
I-- I remember I had a friend.
It was the only one that I met after the war.
He just passed away, Hershel Finkelstein.
He always used to tell me, I won't forget how I was sitting under the window and listening how you were practicing the violin.
And so we lived our life, all the Jews.
And we did whatever it was possible to go on.
We didn't live with it.
Oh, it's [INAUDIBLE] anti-Semitism.
When something happened, it happened.
For instance, my family felt very much anti-Semitism.
It was before I was born.
Or, maybe I was a baby.
I was born in 1917.
And Poland took over in 1918.
So I was maybe a year old.
As I told you, my father was a designer of lady shoes.
So before World War I, he had like a-- it was-- I can't call it a factory.
But it is was in a-- in a smaller form.
But 50 people worked for him.
And he designed, and he gave directions.
And people worked there.
Who were the people?
They were all gentiles.
One Pole worked there, all were Ukrainians.
And when Poland came to-- to be as a country, it was in 1918.
Our home, the first, was pogromed by that Pole who worked for my-- for my father.
So it was a real pogrom.
And-- and we remained with nothing.
I was a baby [INAUDIBLE].
But later, when I was told, that's why my father couldn't make it later big.
So he had the store.
And then it started already the factories that start to make shoes, so custom work wasn't-- so the people couldn't maybe afford more for custom shoes.
I don't know.
But when I was growing up, I know that we had a shoe store.
And-- and one worker who was working, because there were people who wanted, also, shoes to order.
And knowing that my father was a specialist, so he had one worker.
And that time, I remember, he made two pair of shoes a day.
It was quite, quite remarkable.
But we were ruined.
We had to start from the beginning.
So this-- this is Poland for you.
But we went on.
We lived.
And until the war-- it was before-- it was in 1938 it was already.
When Hitler came to-- to Germany, when he took over Germany, it started anti-Semitism.
The Poles, the [INAUDIBLE] they were called, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] took over.
And they did everything that they could to-- to-- to spoil for Jews their living.
And later in '39, before it broke out the war, they didn't let into-- to Jewish stores, they picketed.
And-- and then it was-- it was a [INAUDIBLE].
But we-- we couldn't imagine what awaits us.
Oh, yes.
We-- we knew.
We knew what's going on in Germany.
There-- there was-- it wasn't like-- like television.
Or, radio was a little bit already, but-- but the newspapers.
We were well read.
We were well informed.
What was going on.
And we knew what we have to await.
For instance, our professionals, they couldn't study in-- in Poland.
They have got to go abroad, like doctors, journalists.
Or-- but we had very, very intelligent and able people, Jews, who worked.
For instance, we had a wonderful poet by the name of Julian Tuwim, who was a Polish poet.
But if he would have remained in-- in Poland, he would have ended in Auschwitz.
Luckily, he went out of Poland.
And when he came back-- and while he was creative, he-- he didn't felt himself like a Jew.
It's like the Jews in Germany.
They were Jews of-- of Moses' belief.
You understand?
First they were Germans.
And then there were-- and there they were-- they were Jews of Moses' beliefs.
But it was assimilation like-- like-- like we have it here.
And-- and the same thing was in Poland.
Many, many Jews were assimilated.
And later this same Tuwim came after the war, and then he felt like a Jew.
And he wrote a poem that now I know what it means to be a Jew.
And this is our life.
We learn as we live, and we go on.
But who could imagine or dream in the wildest dream what will happen during the war?
When the time when the war broke out, where were you?
And where was your family?
I was with my-- with my family in Lutsk.
As I told you, I came back from Warsaw, and I remain already.
Because something was in the air, and I wanted to be with my family.
And when Germany went into Europe, and they made the pact with the Soviet Union, so they parted Poland.
In one part, the eastern part, was-- was our part, was Soviets.
And the other part was-- was to the Germans, belonged to the Germans.
But Lutsk came into Soviet rule.
We were two or three days, we were without-- we didn't know who will come in.
This was in 1939.
We didn't know who will come in.
When the war broke out and Germany went in, let's say, to-- to-- to Warsaw and all those parts of Poland, the-- the Soviets came in-- in our part of Poland.
But to Lutsk, they came later, about two days, three days later.
And then they came and started their rule.
So they-- they took away all the stores.
And they sent all the owners to-- to prison.
Luckily, my father, I don't know, they only took away the keys and took away the store and let him go home.
He made an impression.
He knew very well Russian.
And he was a very lovely person.
So he made an impression on them.
I even have pictures.
I didn't show you the picture of my father.
I showed you only in the group.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so we started to live.
The next day when they came in, I-- I stayed in the line about four, five hours to get the bread.
This was there.
But they built up, let's say, a philharmonic.
Ours was on a very high level in Soviet Union.
So to the philharmonic belonged every-- every kind of art.
There was a symphonic orchestra.
They arranged there a choir, that I'll talk to you later about it.
They have a review.
They have soloist, instrumentalists and vocal.
And everybody we came in like to a bureau, like to a factory.
We signed in.
And we shouldn't be late.
Who came a minute late, paid the price.
They paid a fine.
And we were, let's say four hours, and-- and physical workers worked eight hours.
I'll tell you.
There came a lot of refugees from Warsaw, for instance.
When-- when the Germans came and the 6th of September, in 1939, they came into Warsaw.
And-- and there was a rumor that the-- that the-- the men should go away.
So a lot of men fled Warsaw.
And they came wherever they could.
But they came to the Soviet side.
And there came a conductor, a choir conductor, by the name Leon Weiner.
And he came to this philharmonic.
And he wanted to-- to arrange, to-- to form, a choir for the philharmonic.
The philharmonic was a Ukrainian.
So he wanted to-- to-- to form a Ukrainian choir.
And he needed singers.
With me, it happened really-- he had to be a countryman of my sister-in-law.
My brother was married to a lady from Kielce, also a town in Poland that belonged to Soviets.
And he-- he was also from Kielce.
So the only one that he could find, the only house or home that he could find was with my brother.
And he came.
And they happen to be-- to-- to live in Kielce in the same building.
His father was the-- the main cantor of-- and my sister-in-law's father was in-- in coal business.
Because this was the region where it was coal.
And so they were-- they were like-- like family.
And I came in once to my brother.
And he was there, Leon Weiner.
And my brother said, Leon, here is my youngest sister.
She plays the violin, and she sings.
He said, oh, the violin?
I have nothing to do if she wants to go to the orchestra.
But if she sings, and she will pass the exams, I will take her to my choir.
And that's how it happened.
I passed the exams.
And I started to work with Weiner.
